1: Exodus 22 continues the Book of the Covenant (Exodus 20:22–Exodus 23:33), Israel's earliest law code. The ox/sheep theft law opens with a principle found also in the Code of Hammurabi (§8): theft of cattle is punished by a multiplied penalty. The fivefold restitution for an ox and fourfold for a sheep reflect the animal's economic value — oxen were draft animals whose loss disrupted the entire agricultural year, making their theft more damaging. These are case laws (mišpāṭîm), framed as conditional statements ('If anyone does X, then Y'), structurally identical to other ANE legal collections.
2: The night-burglar law is among the most nuanced in the ancient world: killing a thief in the act at night is not murder (no blood-guilt), but killing him during daylight is. The distinction tracks on visibility — at night, there is no way to know if the intruder is a burglar or an assassin; the threat is genuinely life-threatening. By day, the homeowner can see the situation and respond proportionally. Roman law makes a parallel distinction (Twelve Tables 8.12-13). The law demonstrates that even property crimes do not automatically justify lethal force.
3: The 'sold to pay for his theft' clause — if a thief has nothing, he is sold into debt-slavery to make restitution — shows the Book of the Covenant's economic realism. ANE legal collections consistently address the problem of the insolvent debtor; debt-slavery was the standard mechanism for resolving unpayable debt. Leviticus 25 later sets temporal limits (six years maximum, Jubilee release) on such servitude, distinguishing Israelite debt-slavery from chattel slavery. The system presupposes that human dignity survives debt: the thief is sold to work, not permanently owned.
4: The double-restitution principle for found stolen property provides a lesser penalty than the fivefold/fourfold rule of Exodus 22:1, because the animal is still alive — the full economic harm has not occurred. Ancient Near Eastern law collections similarly grade penalties by outcome: the Code of Lipit-Ishtar (§d) and the Laws of Eshnunna (§§12-13) scale theft penalties according to the nature of the property and its recovery. The proportionality principle — penalty calibrated to actual damage — runs throughout the Book of the Covenant.
5: The liability law for grazing damage reflects the agricultural realities of ancient Canaan: fields and vineyards were enclosed by walls or hedges, and livestock straying into a neighbor's crops was a recurring dispute. The principle is clear: the owner of the animal bears responsibility for any damage it causes. Similar liability principles appear in the Laws of Eshnunna (§§53-58) governing water damage and crop damage from breached dikes. The Book of the Covenant is not borrowing from these codes but addresses the same practical realities of agricultural community life.
6: The fire liability law addresses a common hazard in grain-harvest season: fire set to clear stubble or thornbushes that spreads to a neighbor's uncut grain. The dry Palestinian summer (May-October) made such fires genuinely dangerous. The principle of negligence liability — if you started the fire, you pay for damage you could not fully control — reflects a sophisticated understanding of causation in law. The grain harvest setting ties this to the period just before the Feast of Harvest (Exodus 23:16), when such fires would have been most common.
7: The safekeeping law (Exodus 22:7-15) addresses a distinct social practice: entrusting property to a neighbor for temporary custody. In a society without banking institutions, trusted neighbors served as informal depositories. The neighbor who accepts custody assumes a fiduciary responsibility — not absolute liability (if a thief breaks in, the trustee is not responsible), but responsibility to not steal or misappropriate. The appeal 'before God' (hāʾĕlōhîm) for disputed cases likely refers to an oath-taking ceremony at the sanctuary, where YHWH is invoked as the ultimate judge of what cannot be externally verified.
8: 'Must appear before God' (yiqrab ʾel hāʾĕlōhîm) — the Hebrew word translated as 'God' here may refer to divine representatives (judges), as reflected in the LXX's ἐνώπιον τοῦ κριτηρίου τοῦ θεοῦ (before the divine tribunal). The ambiguity reflects the practice of oath-taking before YHWH in difficult cases — the sanctuary or local shrine was the court of appeal when human testimony conflicted. The deterrent effect of a divine oath was significant in a society where supernatural accountability was taken seriously: swearing falsely before YHWH risked divine punishment.
9: The general principle for disputed ownership (Exodus 22:9) extends the adjudication-before-God principle to any contested property — animal, garment, or unspecified lost item claimed by two parties. 'Whom God declares guilty (yĕrašîʿūn ʾĕlōhîm) shall pay double to the other' establishes the proportional penalty for false ownership claims. The doubling penalty for dishonest claims parallels the Code of Hammurabi §120 (where the person who falsely claims another's goods must pay double). The shared penalty structure reflects the universal logic of deterrence: make fraudulent claims costly.
10: The bailment law for animals (Exodus 22:10-13) covers a different category: not storing property but loaning a working animal. Loaned animals are exposed to working risks that a stored item is not — injury, predation, theft. The law distinguishes: YHWH-sworn oath covers death/injury/disappearance with no evidence (Exodus 22:10-11), but theft carries liability because the bailee should have protected it (Exodus 22:12), and predation carries no liability if evidence is produced (Exodus 22:13). This graduated liability system is more sophisticated than the Code of Hammurabi's treatment of herding contracts (§§264-267).
11: The oath before YHWH for contested animal deaths introduces the concept of YHWH as the guarantor of oaths in cases where human verification is impossible. In ANE legal practice, divine oaths were a last resort for cases with no witness — the deity was invoked as the omniscient witness who would punish perjury. Numbers 5:11-31 (the suspected adultery trial) is the most elaborate biblical example of this principle. The theological assumption is that YHWH knows what happened even when humans cannot determine it, making a false oath an act of defiance against divine omniscience.
12: The distinction between an animal stolen from a bailee (bailee liable) and one killed by a predator (bailee not liable) tracks on preventability: theft should have been prevented by proper care, but predation is an unforeseeable force of nature. The same principle underlies modern insurance law's concept of 'act of God' — certain risks are uninsurable because they lie outside human control. The Book of the Covenant's sophistication here reflects centuries of practical community dispute-resolution before being codified.
13: 'If it was torn by a wild animal, let him bring it as evidence (yĕbîʾēhā ʿēd)' — the principle of producing physical evidence to support an exemption claim is consistent with ANE legal practice. The Laws of Eshnunna (§37) similarly require evidence when a stolen animal is claimed to have died. The evidence (the torn remains) makes the claim verifiable rather than relying solely on oath. The combination of physical evidence and oath is the ancient world's two-witness principle — both material and testimonial evidence were required for the most weighty legal exemptions.
14: The animal-borrowing law (Exodus 22:14-15) distinguishes borrowed from hired animals: a borrowed animal is borrowed gratis and its owner is elsewhere, making the borrower fully responsible for any mishap. A hired animal is different — the owner's fee already factors in risk, transferring some liability back to the owner. This reflects economic reasoning: when the owner charges for use, the risk premium is built into the price. The same distinction between gratuitous and compensated bailment runs through Roman law and modern contract law, suggesting this is a universal commercial logic rather than a uniquely Israelite innovation.
15: The seduction of an unbetrothed virgin (Exodus 22:16-17) is treated as a property violation — the bride-price (mohar) is the economic remedy, regardless of whether marriage follows. The bride-price was not a purchase but a gift to the bride's family that established the groom's seriousness and compensated the family for the loss of their daughter. The father's right to refuse the marriage (Exodus 22:17) while still receiving the bride-price acknowledges both his authority over his household and the community's interest in deterring casual sexual exploitation. Deuteronomy 22:28-29 adds that the man must marry the woman and cannot divorce her.
16: See note on Exodus 22:15 — the bride-price law addresses the economic dimension of sexual exploitation. The Code of Hammurabi (§155-156) addresses similar cases with death penalties for some categories; the Book of the Covenant's remedy is economic compensation rather than death, except where the act is aggravated by betrothal (Deuteronomy 22:23-27, where it is treated as assault and potentially capital). The differentiation reflects a nuanced understanding of the range of violation: the unbetrothed case is primarily a property wrong; the betrothed case is analogous to murder because a prior covenant exists.
17: 'Do not allow a sorceress (mĕkaššēpāh) to live' — the prohibition on sorcery appears throughout the ANE: Hammurabi's law code opens with a sorcery case (§2). In Israel's context, divination and magic were associated with Canaanite religion and represented direct competition with YHWH's sovereignty over the future. The term kāšap covers various forms of occult practice. The female form (mĕkaššēpāh) may reflect the reality that women were more commonly associated with such practices in ancient Israelite society, but Deuteronomy 18:10-11 uses inclusive language. Saul's consultation of the medium at Endor (1 Sam 28) illustrates both the prohibition and the sociological reality that such practitioners persisted.
18: Bestiality (šōkēb ʿim bĕhēmāh) is a capital crime in the Book of the Covenant (Exodus 22:19), Leviticus 18:23 and Exodus 20:15-16, and Deuteronomy 27:21. The absolute prohibition distinguishes the Israelite law code from surrounding cultures where mythological accounts of divine-animal unions were part of the cosmological narrative (Egyptian, Canaanite, Greek traditions). The prohibition enforces a categorical boundary between human and animal that is grounded in the creation narrative: humans are made in the divine image (tselem ʾĕlōhîm, Genesis 1:26-27), giving human sexuality a covenantal dignity that excludes animal partners.
19: The death penalty for sacrifice to other gods (Exodus 22:20) is the covenant's most fundamental protection: the Sinai covenant is an exclusive relationship, and any worship directed to another deity is a breach of the fundamental obligation. The verb yāḥāram (devoted/destroyed) is the same root as ḥērem — the total dedication to YHWH through destruction applied to the Canaanite cities in Joshua. The theological principle is that divided loyalty in covenant is not a lesser violation but a category error — like bigamy in a monogamous marriage, it voids the entire relationship.
20: 'Do not mistreat or oppress a foreigner (gēr), for you were foreigners in Egypt' — this is the first statement of the ethical principle that grounds Israelite treatment of the vulnerable in Israel's own formative experience of vulnerability. The gēr (resident alien/foreigner) was a legally vulnerable class — lacking the family networks, property rights, and civic standing that native Israelites possessed. The exodus argument ('you were foreigners in Egypt') is repeated over thirty times in the OT as the motivational basis for social ethics. It is the earliest example in world literature of historical empathy as an ethical foundation.
21: The widow-and-orphan protection clause (Exodus 22:22-24) places these two groups alongside the gēr as the primary triad of the vulnerable. The widow-orphan-stranger triad appears throughout the OT prophets (Isaiah 1:17; Jeremiah 7:6; Zechariah 7:10; Malachi 3:5) and is the defining measure of social justice in Israelite ethics. The Code of Hammurabi's prologue also mentions widows and orphans as those the king protects, but the Israelite version is more radical: YHWH himself is their defender, and mistreating them brings his direct judgment (Exodus 22:23-24).
22: The specific verb used here — ʿinnîtem (afflict, oppress, humble) — is the same verb used for Egypt's oppression of Israel in Exodus 1:11-12. The ethical logic is closing the circle: YHWH freed Israel from being oppressed; therefore Israel must not oppress. The widow and orphan are not merely pitiable objects of charity but persons whose mistreatment is directly analogous to Pharaoh's mistreatment of Israel. James 1:27 identifies care for orphans and widows as the defining mark of pure religion, standing directly in this tradition.
23: 'If you do and they cry out to me (wayyiṣʿăqû ʾēlay)' — the same verb ṣāʿaq is used for Israel's crying out under Egyptian oppression (Exodus 2:23; Exodus 3:7). YHWH's promise to hear the cry of the widow and orphan uses the same language as his hearing Israel's cry in Egypt, making the structural parallel explicit: just as YHWH responded to Israel's cry, he will respond to the cry of those Israel oppresses. This is one of the OT's most direct statements of YHWH's identification with the poor.
24: YHWH's anger aroused to the point of killing with the sword and making the offender's family into the very vulnerable persons he failed to protect (your wives become widows, your children orphans) — this is the lex talionis principle applied at the social level: the oppressor becomes the oppressed. The threat transforms the abstract command into a visceral self-interest argument: if you oppress the vulnerable, you risk making your own family vulnerable. The social ethics here are not merely humanitarian but covenantal consequences enforced by YHWH himself.
25: The prohibition on charging interest (nešek, literally 'bite') to fellow Israelites — specifically the poor — reflects the covenant community's understanding of itself as a family (ʿammî, 'my people') where exploiting the desperate is a betrayal of kinship. Ancient Near Eastern loan contracts routinely charged 20-33% interest on grain loans and silver loans; the Book of the Covenant prohibits this within the covenant community. The distinction between lending within the community and commercial transactions with outsiders reflects the covenantal boundary: economic solidarity applies first within Israel (Deuteronomy 23:19-20 explicitly permits interest from foreigners).
26: The cloak-pledge law addresses a specific form of poverty exploitation: taking a person's outer garment (śalmāh) as collateral for a loan. In the ancient Near East, the outer cloak was a multipurpose garment used both as daily clothing and as a sleeping blanket in the cold Palestinian nights. To hold it overnight was to expose the borrower to genuine physical hardship. The law's return-by-sunset provision is not merely humanitarian but theological: the cloak is not the lender's property to hold at will; it belongs to the covenant community's member whose dignity must be maintained even in debt.
27: 'That cloak is the only covering he has for his body. What else will he sleep in? When he cries out to me, I will hear, for I am compassionate (ḥannûn ʾănî).' The divine self-characterization as ḥannûn (gracious, compassionate) is the same word used in the thirteen attributes of mercy (Exodus 34:6). The connection is deliberate: YHWH's own character is the foundation for the social ethics he requires. God who is compassionate to the poor requires his people to embody the same compassion — this is the 'imitation of God' ethic (imitatio Dei) that becomes explicit in Leviticus 19:2 ('Be holy for I, YHWH your God, am holy').
28: 'Do not blaspheme God (ʾĕlōhîm) or curse the ruler (nāśî) of your people' — the term nāśî (chieftain, prince, one who is lifted up) is used for tribal leaders rather than the later royal term melek (king). This suggests the law's formulation in a pre-monarchic context. Acts 23:5 quotes this verse when Paul apologizes for speaking against the high priest, showing the law's continued authority in Second Temple Judaism. The coupling of divine and civil authority under the same prohibition reflects the covenant's structure: YHWH is king; the nāśî exercises delegated authority; both command respect.
29: The firstfruits and firstborn dedication (Exodus 22:29-30) returns to the theme of Ch.13: everything that 'first opens the womb' or 'first ripens' belongs to YHWH. The agricultural firstfruits (rîʾšîtĕkā — the first of your yield) were presented at the sanctuary as an acknowledgment that the land and its produce are YHWH's gift. 'The firstborn of your sons you shall give to me' — the redemption mechanism (see Exodus 13:12-15; Numbers 18:15-16) means the sons are redeemed by a payment, not literally sacrificed. The law counters the Canaanite practice of child sacrifice by establishing a different mechanism for the firstborn's dedication.
30: 'Seven days the firstborn shall remain with its mother; on the eighth day you shall give it to me' — the seven-day waiting period before presenting the firstborn animal echoes the circumcision law (Genesis 17:12; Leviticus 12:3): the eighth day is the threshold of formal covenant entry. The same structure applies: natural birth (days 1-7) is completed by a covenantal act on the eighth day. This pattern — seven days of natural process followed by an eighth-day covenant marker — is embedded in Israelite ritual: the dedication of the tabernacle (Exodus 29:35-37), the cleansing of a leper (Leviticus 14:8-10), and the ordination of priests (Lev 8-9) all follow it.
31: 'You are to be my holy people (ʿam qādôš)' — this is the covenant community's defining identity statement, appearing here in the context of food law (not eating torn meat). The holiness is not primarily about ritual purity but about covenantal distinctiveness: Israel is set apart to YHWH, and that set-apartness manifests in every domain of life, including diet. The prohibition on eating tĕrēpāh (torn prey) — meat from an animal killed by another animal — is a negative boundary marker: the holy people do not eat what the beasts of the field eat. This holiness-through-boundary-maintenance runs through Leviticus 11-15 and is the foundation of the purity laws.
